name: Tangun
description: Tangun is the legendary founder and god-king of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom. According to the Samguk Yusa and other historical records, Hwanung, the son of Hwanin (the Lord of Heaven), desired to descend to Earth and live among humans. Hwanin granted his wish, bestowing upon him three Heavenly Seals and dispatching him with 3,000 followers to Mount Taebaek (present-day Mount Myohyang). There, Hwanung established Sinsi, a divine city, and governed the world, teaching humans agriculture, medicine, arts, and laws. A bear and a tiger, desiring to become human, prayed to Hwanung. He gave them sacred mugwort and garlic and instructed them to avoid sunlight for 100 days. The tiger failed, but the bear persevered and transformed into a woman named Ungnyeo. Ungnyeo prayed to conceive a child, and Hwanung, moved by her devotion, transformed himself and impregnated her. She gave birth to Tangun, who later founded Gojoseon and reigned as its first king, establishing the Korean people's ancestral lineage and their connection to the divine. Tangun's reign is said to have lasted for 1,500 years, after which he abdicated and became a mountain god.
subtitle: The Founder King of Gojoseon

keyMyths.title: The Descent of Hwanung
keyMyths.description: Hwanung, son of the Lord of Heaven, descends to Earth with 3,000 followers to civilize humanity. He establishes Sinsi on Mount Taebaek and teaches humans essential skills and knowledge.

keyMyths.title: The Bear and the Tiger
keyMyths.description: A bear and a tiger seek to become human through Hwanung's intervention. The bear succeeds, transforming into Ungnyeo, while the tiger fails to endure the trial.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Tangun
keyMyths.description: Ungnyeo prays for a child, and Hwanung transforms and impregnates her, resulting in the birth of Tangun. Tangun becomes the founder and first king of Gojoseon.

sources.author: Il-yeon
sources.title: Samguk Yusa
sources.description: A 13th-century collection of legends and historical accounts relating to the Three Kingdoms of Korea, containing the primary narrative of Tangun's origin and the founding of Gojoseon.

sources.author: Lee, Peter H.
sources.title: Sourcebook of Korean Civilization, Vol. 1: From Early Times to the Sixteenth Century
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of primary sources relating to Korean history and culture, providing valuable context and analysis of the Tangun myth and its significance.

associations.name: Bear
associations.significance: Represents endurance, transformation, and the earthly origins of the Korean people. The bear's transformation into a woman symbolizes the integration of nature and humanity.

associations.name: Tiger
associations.significance: Represents courage, strength, and the challenges faced in achieving enlightenment. The tiger's failure to transform highlights the difficulty of transcending primal nature.

associations.name: Mount Taebaek
associations.significance: A sacred place of spiritual power and transformation, where Hwanung descended and Tangun was born, symbolizing the connection between heaven and earth.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: The three Heavenly Seals represent authority and divine mandate, while the three deities of wind, rain, and clouds symbolize Hwanung's control over nature.

associations.name: Mugwort and Garlic
associations.significance: Represent purification, healing, and the potential for transformation. Their consumption by the bear and tiger signifies a test of will and perseverance.

associations.name: Sunlight
associations.significance: Symbolizes enlightenment, divine blessing, and the power to overcome darkness. It represents the hope for a prosperous future under Tangun's rule.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are considered founding figures of their respective civilizations, with connections to divine ancestry and the establishment of societal order.
Both are legendary first emperors with divine lineage, establishing a royal dynasty and shaping the cultural identity of their nations.
Like Hwanin, Odin is a sky father figure who influences earthly affairs and bestows blessings upon humanity.

worshipPractices.name: Tangun Shrine Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Traditional rituals are performed at Tangun shrines, especially on National Foundation Day (Gaecheonjeol), to honor Tangun's legacy and seek blessings for the nation's prosperity. These rituals often involve offerings of food, wine, and prayers.

worshipPractices.name: National Foundation Day Celebrations
worshipPractices.description: Gaecheonjeol, celebrated annually on October 3rd, commemorates the legendary founding of Gojoseon by Tangun. It is a national holiday marked by parades, cultural performances, and patriotic displays, reinforcing national identity and pride.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tangun remains a potent symbol of Korean national identity, unity, and cultural heritage. His story is taught in schools, referenced in popular culture, and invoked in political discourse to foster a sense of shared history and destiny among Koreans.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Divine Descent
extendedContent.content: According to legend, Tangun's lineage traces back to Hwanin, the Lord of Heaven. Hwanin's son, Hwanung, yearned to descend to Earth and bring civilization to humanity. Granted permission, Hwanung descended to Mount Taebaek with 3,000 followers, establishing a divine city called Sinsi. There, he governed with benevolence, teaching humans agriculture, medicine, and the arts. A bear and a tiger sought to become human, and Hwanung gave them mugwort and garlic, instructing them to avoid sunlight. The tiger failed, but the bear persevered, transforming into a woman named Ungnyeo. Ungnyeo prayed for a child, and Hwanung, moved by her devotion, took her as his wife. She gave birth to Tangun, who inherited both divine and earthly qualities, setting the stage for his future role as the founder of Gojoseon.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Founding of Gojoseon
extendedContent.content: Tangun's most significant quest was the establishment of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom. After being born to Ungnyeo and Hwanung, Tangun grew into a wise and benevolent leader. At the age of 19, he ascended to the throne and established his capital at Asadal, marking the beginning of Gojoseon. He ruled with justice and compassion, promoting agriculture, weaving, and other essential skills. He emphasized moral principles and a harmonious relationship between humans and nature. Tangun's reign is characterized by peace and prosperity, laying the foundation for Korean civilization. Under his guidance, Gojoseon flourished, expanding its territory and influence throughout the Korean peninsula. His reign lasted for over a thousand years, shaping the cultural and spiritual identity of the Korean people.

extendedContent.title: Enduring Legacy and Symbolism
extendedContent.content: Tangun's legacy extends far beyond his legendary reign, shaping the cultural and spiritual identity of Korea for millennia. He is revered as the progenitor of the Korean people, embodying the values of benevolence, wisdom, and harmony. His story serves as a foundational myth, providing a sense of shared history and cultural unity. Tangun's influence is evident in Korean art, literature, and folklore, where he is often depicted as a wise and benevolent ruler. National Foundation Day (Gaecheonjeol) is celebrated annually to commemorate the founding of Gojoseon by Tangun, reinforcing national pride and identity. The story of Tangun continues to inspire and resonate with Koreans, serving as a reminder of their rich cultural heritage and enduring spirit. His name and image are often invoked in political and social contexts to promote national unity and patriotism.

shortDescription: Tangun is the legendary founder and god-king of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom. He is a symbol of Korean national identity and cultural heritage.
